messages.content:
Please modify the content: Once you register as a M.Sc. student you will be given an identity card. You should carry the identity card at all times when you are in the Faculty. If you lose the identity card you need to provide a police report to obtain another identity card. You will be charged for the new identity card. 
You are required to return the identity card to the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology once you sit for the final examination.

Upon enrollment as a M.Sc. student, an identity card will be issued to you. It is mandatory to have the identity card with you whenever you are present within the Faculty premises. In the unfortunate event that you misplace the identity card, you must acquire a police report to receive a replacement. Please note that there will be a fee associated with obtaining a new identity card.

Kindly ensure that you return the identity card to the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology after completing the final examination.

Please modify this section: Dress code
Students are required to dress modestly and decently. Long hair should be tied back, especially in the laboratories. Rubber slippers, shorts and mini-skirts are not acceptable.   

Student representative 
The students are required to select a representative who will liaise with the course coordinator regarding matters relating to the course.

Dress Code:
It is expected that students adhere to a modest and decent dress code. For safety reasons, long hair should be tied back, particularly when working in laboratories. Attire such as rubber slippers, shorts, and mini-skirts are not considered appropriate.

Student Representative:
Each student group is responsible for choosing a representative who will serve as a point of communication between the students and the course coordinator, addressing any course-related concerns or inquiries.

Please modify this statement: In case of illness you should supply a doctor’s certificate or, if the illness is brief, a self-
certification. If you are absent for other reasons then you should write a letter to the

Programme Director explaining the circumstances. Medical certificates or letters should
be given in person or sent to the Programme Administrator. Whatever your reason for
being away, tell your supervisor about it and make any necessary arrangements to catch
up with work you have missed.

If you are unable to attend due to illness, you must provide a doctor's certificate. In cases of a short-term illness, a self-certification may be submitted. However, if you are absent for reasons other than illness, it is necessary to write a letter to the Programme Director, detailing the circumstances that led to your absence. 

Medical certificates or letters should be either delivered in person or sent to the Programme Administrator. Regardless of the reason for your absence, it is important to inform your supervisor and make appropriate arrangements to make up for any missed work.

Modify this part: Plagiarism is the use of the intellectual work of other people, without acknowledgement. In other words, it is the presentation of another person’s thoughts or words as though they were your own.

All work submitted as part of the requirements for any examination must be expressed in your own words and incorporate your own ideas and judgments. Care should be taken to avoid plagiarism, particularly in reports written in your own time. 

You are encouraged to read and criticize the work of others as much as possible and expected to incorporate this in your thinking and in your reports and dissertations. However, it is essential that you acknowledge your sources. 

Direct quotations from published or unpublished work of others, from the internet or from any other source, they must always be clearly referred to, at each instance. If you summarize another person’s ideas, judgments, figures, diagrams or tables, the sources must similarly be referred to at each instance. A full reference to the source must be provided in the proper form, in the reference list.

Plagiarism is defined as the unauthorized use of someone else's intellectual property, such as thoughts or words, without giving appropriate credit. In simpler terms, it involves presenting another person's work as your own.

When submitting any work as part of an examination requirement, it is crucial that you express your ideas and judgments using your own words. It is imperative to actively avoid plagiarism, especially when working on reports during your own time.

You are strongly encouraged to engage in extensive reading and critique of others' work, and it is expected that you incorporate these perspectives into your thinking, reports, and dissertations. However, it is essential to acknowledge the sources you have drawn upon.

When directly quoting published or unpublished work, whether from the internet or any other source, proper references must be provided. If you summarize someone else's ideas, judgments, figures, diagrams, or tables, the sources must be cited accordingly. A comprehensive reference list with the proper format should be included to attribute credit to the sources used.

Modify this content: Several computers are available in the Department for use by M.Sc. students. All your documents should be taken into pen drivers or CDs and should not be saved into the hard disks. Certain computers are dedicated for use with certain equipment (eg: HPLC machine, Gel documentation system, micro-plate readers) and should not be used for any other purpose.

The Department provides numerous computers exclusively for the use of M.Sc. students. It is important to note that all your documents should be saved on pen drives or CDs and should not be stored on the hard disks of these computers.

Please be aware that specific computers are designated for use with certain equipment, such as the HPLC machine, Gel documentation system, and micro-plate readers. These computers must not be utilized for any other purposes besides their assigned tasks.

Modify this: A dedicated library of general and specialized text books are available for your use in the Department. Additionally you will have access to reference books and journals available at the Faculty of Medicine library.

The Department offers a dedicated library that consists of both general and specialized textbooks for your utilization. This collection is designed to support your academic needs effectively. 

Furthermore, you will have access to an extensive range of reference books and journals, which can be found at the library of the Faculty of Medicine. This additional resource will provide you with comprehensive materials for further research and study.

Please modify this: Wifi Internet facilities are available through the Faculty server to most computers in the Department. Internet points are available in the computer room as well as the M.Sc Lecture room and laboratories.

The Department provides access to Wifi Internet facilities through the Faculty server, allowing connectivity to most computers in the Department. Internet points are conveniently located in key areas such as the computer room, M.Sc. Lecture room, and laboratories for your convenience and ease of access.

Modify this: The Department provides access to Wifi Internet facilities through the Faculty server, allowing connectivity to your computers and tabs. Lecture room, and laboratories are eqippied with wifi internet free of charge.

The Department offers Wifi Internet access through the Faculty server, enabling connectivity for your computers and tablets. You can enjoy complimentary access to wifi internet in the lecture rooms and laboratories, ensuring seamless connectivity for your academic pursuits.

Modify this: An ideal length of the dissertation is ~20,000 words, which equals to ~80 pages. With the inclusion of the figures and tables, references etc, the total length is likely to be in the region of 100-150 pages.

The recommended length for the dissertation is approximately 20,000 words, equivalent to around 80 pages. It should be noted that this estimate accounts for the inclusion of figures, tables, references, and other relevant elements. Consequently, the final length of the dissertation is expected to fall within the range of 100-150 pages.

Modify this : The dissertation should be word-processed and printed single-side only, on A4 size paper. 
Margins: 1.25 inches on left and top and 1.00 inch on right and bottom
Font: Times New Roman, size 12
Line spacing: 1.5 line spaced
-	should be used throughout, including, abstract, acknowledgments, text, Tables and references. 
Text alignment: justify
Page numbering: All pages should be numbered consecutively throughout the thesis beginning with the abstract page, in Arabic numerals and should be inserted in the bottom middle. 
Tables and Figures:  should be placed as near as possible to the appropriate text. Each should be numbered serially in Arabic numerals and should be have a heading and a legend. (Maps, graphs, photographs should be labeled as figures).

The dissertation should be prepared using word-processing software and printed on A4 size paper, strictly on a single side. Pay close attention to the following formatting guidelines:

Margins: Maintain a margin of 1.25 inches on the left and top, and 1.00 inch on the right and bottom of each page.

Font: Use Times New Roman with a font size of 12 for the entire dissertation, including the abstract, acknowledgments, main text, tables, and references.

Line spacing: Maintain a 1.5 line spacing throughout the entire dissertation.

Text alignment: Justify the alignment of the text.

Page numbering: All pages, beginning with the abstract page, should be consecutively numbered using Arabic numerals. The page numbers should be inserted at the bottom center of each page.

Tables and Figures: Place tables and figures as close to the relevant text as possible. Number each table and figure sequentially using Arabic numerals, and provide a clear heading and legend for each one. Please note that figures may include maps, graphs, and photographs, which should be labeled appropriately as figures.
